items.name: DeepSeek reasonix
items.source_story_title: DeepSeek reasonix, DeepSeek native coding agent with high caching and low cost
items.summary: A low-cost coding agent with aggressive context caching.
items.why_included: Commenters kept comparing it favorably with other coding agents and cheap model choices.
items.evidence: HN comments said DeepSeek V4 Pro was a preferred cheap model and paired DeepSeek with opencode and Claude Code.

items.name: Firefox Adafruit Web Serial
items.source_story_title: Build Adafruit projects right from Firefox
items.summary: A Firefox Web Serial flow for building Adafruit projects.
items.why_included: Commenters called it a genuinely useful beginner-friendly browser feature.
items.evidence: One commenter said “Great to see Firefox getting on board,” and another called it an “Amazing feature for beginners.”

items.name: Handbook of Graphic Presentation
items.source_story_title: I spent 50 hours drawing a line graph
items.summary: A classic data-graphics handbook on clearer charts.
items.why_included: A commenter explicitly recommended adding it to the list as a useful reference.
items.evidence: The thread included “You should add in Calvin Schmid’s Handbook of Graphic Presentation into your list.”

items.name: Mastering Dyalog APL
items.summary: A free Dyalog APL book for learning array programming.
items.why_included: Commenters described it as a motivating way to ramp into APL.
items.evidence: One comment said it gave “dopamine hits” and was a nice way to ramp up, while others linked Dyalog editor resources and BQN docs.

items.name: Usborne 1980s Computer Books
items.summary: A free archive of nostalgic beginner-friendly computer books.
items.why_included: The thread was full of childhood memories and praise for the books’ clarity.
items.evidence: Commenters praised the “clear instruction and evocative old-school art” and said the BASIC books started their coding journey.

items.name: Ruby for Good
items.summary: A friendly Ruby event for maintaining real open-source projects.
items.why_included: The comments made it sound like a hands-on community gathering rather than a generic conference.
items.evidence: The founder said it is “absolutely not a hackathon,” and a volunteer said they had “a great experience.”

items.name: 86-DOS version 0.1-C
items.source_story_title: Microsoft open-sources "the earliest DOS source code discovered to date"
items.summary: An original 86-DOS disk image, the MS-DOS precursor.
items.why_included: Commenters highlighted it as an even earlier surviving release that you can still run.
items.evidence: One comment noted the 0.1-C binaries are earlier than the source release and can be downloaded and used in an emulator.

items.name: BQN
items.summary: A modern APL-style array language with a quick-start guide.
items.why_included: It surfaced as a concrete follow-on resource for the APL learning thread.
items.evidence: A commenter linked the BQN repo and quick guide alongside other APL editor resources.
